evals.name: Repeating Arguments with Name-Value Options
evals.description: Tests whether the agent uses arguments (Repeating) + two-block pattern instead of varargin
evals.prompt: Write a MATLAB function called overlaySignals that accepts repeating pairs of (time, signal) data followed by optional name-value arguments for Title (string, default ""), YLabel (string, default "Amplitude"), and ShowGrid (logical, default true). The function should plot all signals overlaid on a single figure. Use modern argument validation.

assertions.description: Uses arguments (Repeating) block
assertions.expected: arguments (Repeating)

assertions.description: Generated code does not use varargin
assertions.expected: The generated function code does not declare or use varargin in its signature or body. It uses arguments (Repeating) for the repeating inputs. (Mentions of varargin in surrounding prose or comments — e.g. 'instead of varargin' — do not violate this; the check is on the generated function code itself.)

assertions.description: Uses two-block pattern: Repeating block followed by a plain arguments block for name-value options
assertions.expected: Two separate arguments blocks: first with (Repeating), second without for name-value options

assertions.description: Accesses repeating variables as cell arrays with curly-brace indexing
assertions.expected: Loop uses {i} indexing on the repeating variables (e.g., time{i}, signal{i})

assertions.description: Generated function runs without error

evals.name: Constructor with .?ClassName Property Import
evals.description: Tests whether the agent uses .?ClassName instead of redeclaring properties
evals.prompt: Write a MATLAB class called NetworkConfig with properties: Host (1,1) string = "localhost", Port (1,1) double {mustBePositive, mustBeInteger} = 8080, Timeout (1,1) double {mustBePositive} = 30, Protocol (1,1) string {mustBeMember(Protocol, ["http","https","ws"])} = "https". Write a constructor that accepts all properties as name-value arguments.

assertions.description: Uses .?ClassName syntax
assertions.expected: .?NetworkConfig

assertions.description: Does not redeclare property validation in the arguments block
assertions.expected: The arguments block contains only nvArgs.?NetworkConfig (and possibly overrides), not full redeclarations of Host, Port, Timeout, Protocol with their sizes and validators

assertions.description: Uses fieldnames + loop pattern to assign properties
assertions.expected: Constructor body uses fieldnames(nvArgs) and a loop to assign properties to obj

assertions.description: Class can be instantiated with name-value arguments

evals.name: Migration from inputParser with @ischar
evals.description: Tests whether the agent preserves the original contract when migrating @ischar validation

assertions.description: Uses char or string for filename — not mustBeTextScalar (which also accepts cellstr)
assertions.expected: filename uses (1,:) char, (1,1) string, or mustBeA — any of these are acceptable migrations from @ischar

assertions.description: MaxRetries uses mustBeNumeric or no class spec — not double (which would convert)
assertions.expected: MaxRetries does not have a double class spec without mustBeNumeric, since @isnumeric rejects non-numeric but the double class spec would silently convert

assertions.description: Uses arguments block
assertions.expected: arguments

assertions.description: Migrated function executes without error

evals.name: Size Spec Semantics Review
evals.description: Tests whether the agent correctly understands that size specs reshape rather than reject
evals.prompt:
I have this MATLAB function signature:

function result = processVector(data)
    arguments
        data (1,:) double
    end
    result = sum(data);
end

What happens when I call processVector([1; 2; 3]) — passing a column vector? Does it error, or does it succeed? Explain the behavior and suggest how to make it reject column vectors if that's what I want.

assertions.description: Correctly states that (1,:) reshapes the column to a row (does NOT error)
assertions.expected: The response states that passing a column vector to (1,:) does NOT produce an error — it silently reshapes the column to a row vector

assertions.description: Recommends mustBeRow to reject columns
assertions.expected: Suggests using mustBeRow validator (not a size spec) to actually reject column vectors

assertions.description: Does not incorrectly claim the call will error
assertions.expected: The response does NOT claim that processVector([1; 2; 3]) produces an error or exception. It correctly states the call succeeds silently by reshaping the column vector to a row. (Phrasings like 'no error', 'does NOT error', or 'will error if you use mustBeRow' are correct and acceptable; what's wrong is asserting the original call as written produces an error.)

evals.name: Class Spec Conversion Awareness
evals.description: Tests whether the agent understands class specs convert rather than reject

assertions.description: Correctly identifies that char is silently converted to double (ASCII codes)
assertions.expected: The response explains that 'hello' will be converted to its ASCII double values [104 101 108 108 111] because the double class spec converts rather than rejects

assertions.description: Suggests mustBeNumeric or mustBeA to prevent the conversion
assertions.expected: Recommends adding mustBeNumeric (without class spec) or mustBeA(data, 'double') to reject non-numeric input

assertions.description: Response names a concrete validator from the skill (deterministic anchor)
assertions.expected: mustBe

evals.name: Auto-Trigger on Input-Handling Phrasing
evals.description: Tests whether the skill activates on natural-language prompts that ask to improve input handling without using arguments-block keywords. Reproduces zachlevy's report on issue #3 (svd.m).

assertions.description: Response uses an arguments block
assertions.expected: arguments

assertions.description: Response uses at least one mustBe validator
assertions.expected: mustBe

assertions.description: Adds size or class constraints to start, stop, and n
assertions.expected: Each argument gets at least one of: a size spec like (1,1), a class spec, or a validator. n in particular is constrained to a positive integer (mustBePositive, mustBeInteger, or equivalent)

evals.name: Auto-Trigger on Harden Phrasing (.?ClassName)
evals.description: Tests trigger via 'harden this constructor'. Class has several properties already validated; baseline tends to redeclare them in the constructor, which the skill teaches to avoid via .?ClassName.
evals.prompt:
Can you harden this constructor? I want the inputs to be properly validated.

classdef SensorConfig
    properties
        SampleRate (1,1) double {mustBePositive} = 1000
        Resolution (1,1) double {mustBePositive, mustBeInteger} = 16
        FilterOrder (1,1) double {mustBePositive, mustBeInteger} = 4
        Label (1,1) string = "unnamed"
    end
    methods
        function obj = SensorConfig(sampleRate, resolution, filterOrder, label)
            obj.SampleRate = sampleRate;
            obj.Resolution = resolution;
            obj.FilterOrder = filterOrder;
            obj.Label = label;
        end
    end
end

assertions.description: Constructor uses .?SensorConfig syntax
assertions.expected: .?SensorConfig

assertions.description: Constructor does not redeclare property validators
assertions.expected: The arguments block contains nvArgs.?SensorConfig (and possibly overrides), not full redeclarations like options.SampleRate (1,1) double {mustBePositive} = 1000

assertions.description: Constructor body assigns properties via fieldnames + loop, not per-property
assertions.expected: Body uses fieldnames(nvArgs) and a for loop with obj.(props{i}) = nvArgs.(props{i}) — not explicit obj.SampleRate = nvArgs.SampleRate; obj.Resolution = ...

evals.name: Auto-Trigger on Robustness Phrasing (varargin)
evals.description: Tests trigger via 'make this more robust'. Function uses varargin so the agent should migrate to arguments (Repeating).

assertions.description: Response uses arguments (Repeating)
assertions.expected: arguments (Repeating)

assertions.description: Rewritten code does not retain varargin
assertions.expected: The rewritten function code does not declare or use varargin in its signature or body. It uses arguments (Repeating) with separately declared cell variables (e.g., x, y). Mentions of varargin in surrounding prose, comments, or quoted snippets of the original function (e.g. 'replacing varargin with arguments (Repeating)') do not violate this — the check is on the rewritten function code itself.

assertions.description: Loop accesses repeating variables with curly-brace indexing on separately declared variables
assertions.expected: The for loop uses {i} indexing on individually named cell variables (e.g., x{i}, y{i}) — not args{k}/args{k+1} on a single bundle

evals.name: Skill-Body Invocation on Trivial-Looking Prompt (mustBeRow)
evals.description: Tests whether the agent consults the skill *body* (not just discovery via description) when the prompt looks superficially simple but the correct answer requires gotcha knowledge taught only inside the skill. Reproduces jakew's report on issue #14. The discriminator is whether the agent uses `mustBeRow` (skill-body knowledge: `(1,:)` reshapes columns silently) vs `(1,:)` (default heuristic; semantically wrong for a `Reject column vectors` requirement).
evals.prompt: Write a MATLAB function rowSum(v) that takes a row vector and returns its sum. Reject column vectors. Use modern argument validation.

assertions.description: Uses mustBeRow validator — does not rely on (1,:) size spec for rejection
assertions.expected: Validators include mustBeRow (R2024b+). A bare (1,:) size spec is wrong because it silently reshapes column vectors into rows instead of rejecting them, contradicting the explicit 'Reject column vectors' requirement.

assertions.description: Does not claim that (1,:) alone rejects column vectors
assertions.expected: Response must not claim that (1,:) size spec rejects, errors on, or is incompatible with a column vector — that claim is empirically false.

assertions.description: Uses arguments block
assertions.expected: arguments

evals.name: Complex-Number Rejection in Numeric Contracts
evals.description: Tests whether the agent adds mustBeReal when complex inputs are nonsensical (sizes, indices, counts). Reproduces kkline's report on issue #10. Baseline on the bare Hilbert prompt typically uses {mustBeInteger, mustBePositive} only — which accepts 1+0i silently because complex values pass mustBeInteger when imaginary part is zero, and worse, complex-with-imag passes mustBeInteger if both real and imag are integer-valued.

assertions.description: Validators include mustBeReal
assertions.expected: mustBeReal

assertions.description: Validators on n include all of mustBeInteger, mustBePositive, and mustBeReal
assertions.expected: n is constrained with mustBeInteger and mustBePositive (so it's a positive integer) AND mustBeReal (so complex values are rejected — 1+2i passes mustBeNumeric/mustBeFinite/mustBeInteger silently otherwise)

assertions.description: Uses arguments block
assertions.expected: arguments

evals.name: validatestring Preservation on Migration
evals.description: Tests whether the agent preserves validatestring semantics when migrating to an arguments block. Reproduces kkline's report on issue #17. validatestring does case-insensitive prefix matching ('AU' -> 'auto') and returns the canonical form; mustBeMember is exact-match only. The behavior-preserving migration keeps validatestring inside the body and uses the arguments block only for the type/size guard.

assertions.description: Retains the validatestring call in the migrated function body
assertions.expected: validatestring

assertions.description: Does not replace validatestring with mustBeMember as the primary recommendation
assertions.expected: The migrated code keeps validatestring(mode, {...}) inside the body. If the response also presents a mustBeMember alternative, it must explicitly flag that this alternative breaks behavior on case/prefix variants like 'AU' or 'AUTO'.

assertions.description: Uses arguments block with a text validator (mustBeTextScalar) or a (1,:) char / (1,1) string spec
assertions.expected: arguments

evals.name: validateattributes Migration with Reshape and Conversion Gotchas
evals.description: Tests whether the agent preserves the contract when migrating validateattributes that uses {'double'} (rejects non-double) and {'size',[1,NaN]} (rejects columns). Baseline tends to use `double` class spec (converts) and `(1,:)` size spec (reshapes), both of which broaden the contract silently.

assertions.description: row uses mustBeA(row, "double") — not double class spec, which converts non-double numeric silently
assertions.expected: row's validators include mustBeA(row, "double") (or equivalent strict rejection). A bare `double` class spec is wrong because validateattributes {'double'} rejects non-double, while the class spec would convert.

assertions.description: row uses mustBeRow — not (1,:) size spec, which silently reshapes columns
assertions.expected: row's validators include mustBeRow (R2024b+). A `(1,:)` size spec is wrong because validateattributes {'size',[1,NaN]} rejects column vectors, while (1,:) silently reshapes them.

assertions.description: Range check uses mustBeGreaterThanOrEqual/mustBeLessThanOrEqual or mustBeBetween
assertions.expected: threshold's validators include either mustBeGreaterThanOrEqual(threshold, 0) + mustBeLessThanOrEqual(threshold, 1), or mustBeBetween(threshold, 0, 1, "closed") (R2025a+)

assertions.description: Uses arguments block
assertions.expected: arguments

evals.name: Tilde Placeholder Preservation in Class Method
evals.description: Tests whether the agent preserves the ~ placeholder when adding an arguments block to a class method. Reproduces the failure mode in issue #15 (nstefans, 2026-05-27): at xhigh thinking, the model rewrites `function normalize(~, results)` into `function normalize(obj, results)` with `obj (1,1) ClassName` and a %#ok<INUSA> pragma — broadening the contract and reintroducing the lint warning the original ~ was suppressing.
evals.prompt: add argument block to the normalize method in MetricCalculator.m

context.setup: MetricCalculator.m contains a classdef with a `function normalize(~, results) %#ok<INUSL>` method that uses the unused class instance placeholder. Copy fixtures/MetricCalculator.m into the working directory before running the prompt.

assertions.description: Function signature still uses ~ placeholder for the unused class instance
assertions.expected: The function header reads `function normalize(~, results)` — the ~ is NOT renamed to obj/this/self/_ or any other identifier.

assertions.description: Arguments block uses ~ as the placeholder, with no class/size spec
assertions.expected: The arguments block contains a bare `~` line (no type spec, no validators) for the first argument. A line like `obj (1,1) MetricCalculator` for the first argument is wrong because it requires a name and forces a rename.

assertions.description: No %#ok<INUSA> / %#ok<MANU> pragma added to the method or arguments block
assertions.expected: No new mlint suppression pragmas appear. The original %#ok<INUSL> on the function header may stay (harmless) or be removed. New %#ok<INUSA> on the arguments-block name or %#ok<MANU> on the ~ are both wrong — they only become necessary because the model renamed ~ to a real identifier.

assertions.description: Uses arguments block
assertions.expected: arguments

evals.name: Repeating Output Arguments — Single-Name Constraint
evals.description: Tests whether the agent knows that arguments (Output, Repeating) only allows ONE name in the block. Reproduces issue #12 (2026-05-27): for a function returning N*3 outputs (product, factor1, factor2 triples), the model writes `arguments (Output, Repeating) p; a; b; end` which fails to parse with MATLAB:functionValidation:MultipleRepeatingOutputs. The correct pattern is a single-name block plus packing convention.
evals.prompt: write a MATLAB function file in the current directory which uses a repeating output arguments block. the function should allow requesting N*3 outputs for any integer N >= 0. For any N >= 0, the Nth value should be equal to (N+1th value)*(N+2th value). The values for the N+1th and N+2th outputs must lie within the range [0,1] but are otherwise unconstrained.

assertions.description: Uses arguments (Output, Repeating) block
assertions.expected: arguments (Output, Repeating)

assertions.description: Repeating output block declares exactly one name
assertions.expected: The `arguments (Output, Repeating)` block contains a single argument-name declaration (one variable). Multi-name declarations like `p (1,1) double; a (1,1) double; b (1,1) double` are wrong — MATLAB rejects them with `MultipleRepeatingOutputs`.

assertions.description: Function parses and executes; calling with 6 outputs returns triples where each first element equals the product of the next two
assertions.expected: When called as [p1,a1,b1,p2,a2,b2] = <fnname>(); the function returns successfully and abs(p1 - a1*b1) < 1e-12 and abs(p2 - a2*b2) < 1e-12. No `MultipleRepeatingOutputs` parse error.

assertions.description: Factor outputs are validated to lie in [0, 1]
assertions.expected: Either the repeating-output validator on the single declared name covers [0,1] (e.g. mustBeInRange or mustBeGreaterThanOrEqual + mustBeLessThanOrEqual) OR the body uses rand() (which is in [0,1] by construction) AND no factor escapes [0,1].

evals.name: Maximal Argument-Block Composition
evals.description: Tests whether the agent knows that required + optional + ignored + repeating + ignored repeating + name-value + .?ClassName all compose legally in one function. Reproduces issue #13 (jakew, 2026-05-26): when asked for the full combination, the model fabricated the constraint `MATLAB does not allow optional positional inputs (args with defaults) in the same function as arguments (Repeating). They're mutually exclusive` — false. The named-class slot uses `matlab.graphics.chart.primitive.Bar` (which has many public-settable properties) so the .?ClassName import has real settable name-value args to demonstrate; using a class with no public-settable properties (e.g. onCleanup) creates a silent-positional-fallback trap when callers pass name-value args against it.
evals.prompt: use /matlab-validate-function-arguments to write a function which has both required positional inputs, optional positional inputs, ignored positional inputs, repeating inputs, ignored repeating inputs, name value inputs, and named class inputs (use matlab.graphics.chart.primitive.Bar as the class name). I don't care what the function does. It can disp each argument received for all I care

assertions.description: The generated function parses and is callable with arguments covering all categories
assertions.expected: Calling the generated function with at least one required arg, one optional arg, one ignored positional, two repeating positional groups, name-value pairs, and a Bar property name-value pair (e.g. FaceColor=[1 0 0]) must succeed with no parse-time errors (no MATLAB:functionValidation:* errors).

assertions.description: Does not falsely claim optional positional inputs and (Repeating) are mutually exclusive
assertions.expected: The response does not falsely claim that optional positional inputs and `arguments (Repeating)` cannot coexist in the same function. Quoting the SKILL.md "Common mistakes" table cell that explicitly states they compose legally does NOT violate this — the check is on the agent's own claims about the language, not on quoted skill content. Likewise, the agent describing the order constraint (required → optional → repeating → name-value) is fine. The assertion fails only if the agent asserts on its own that these categories cannot appear in the same function or in the same arguments block.

assertions.description: Function body actually contains all requested categories
assertions.expected: The function definition includes: at least one required arg (no default), at least one optional arg (with =default), at least one ~ placeholder in a plain block, an arguments (Repeating) block containing both a named arg and a ~ placeholder (or an equivalent ignored repeating), at least one name-value arg with explicit type, and a `.?matlab.graphics.chart.primitive.Bar` (or `.?<struct>matlab.graphics.chart.primitive.Bar`) name-value import. Skipping any category to dodge the question is wrong.

assertions.description: Block ordering follows required → optional → repeating → name-value across all blocks
assertions.expected: Across all `arguments` blocks combined, the argument categories appear in order: required positionals, optional positionals (with defaults), repeating, name-value. No required-after-optional, optional-after-repeating, or positional-after-named violations.

evals.name: Forwarding name-value options with namedargs2cell
evals.description: Tests whether the agent uses `namedargs2cell` to forward a validated name-value options struct to another function in one expression, rather than building the forwarded cell manually with `isfield`/conditionals.
evals.prompt: Write a MATLAB function `wrapPlot(x, y, opts)` that wraps the built-in `plot` function. It should validate `x` and `y` as 1-D numeric row vectors. It should accept any subset of these name-value options and forward them to `plot`: `LineWidth` (positive scalar, default 1), `Color` (1-by-3 RGB row in [0,1], default [0 0.4 0.7]), `LineStyle` (string scalar, default "-"). Use a single options struct in the arguments block and forward only the options to `plot` in one call.

assertions.description: Uses namedargs2cell to convert opts to a forwardable cell
assertions.expected: namedargs2cell

assertions.description: Forwards options to plot with a single call using cell expansion
assertions.expected: The function calls `plot` once and forwards the options as expanded cell-array elements (e.g., `plot(x, y, args{:})` after `args = namedargs2cell(opts)`). The function does NOT use a chain of `if isfield(opts, ...)` or per-option conditionals to decide whether to pass each option.

assertions.description: Options are grouped under a single struct name in the arguments block
assertions.expected: The arguments block declares the three options as fields of one struct (e.g., `opts.LineWidth`, `opts.Color`, `opts.LineStyle`) — not as three separate top-level name-value entries that would each require independent forwarding logic.

assertions.description: Function executes without error when called with a subset of options

evals.name: Migration that requires rejection (not class-spec conversion)
evals.description: Tests whether the agent preserves the original rejection contract when migrating an isa-based check. The class-spec trap (using `double` as a class spec, which silently converts single/char) must be avoided; the correct migration uses `mustBeA(x, "double")`.

assertions.description: Uses an arguments block
assertions.expected: arguments

assertions.description: Does not use a `double` class spec on values (which would silently convert single/char instead of rejecting)
assertions.expected: The validation for `values` does NOT include a bare `double` class specifier in the position before `{...}` (e.g., `values double {...}` or `values (..) double {...}`). The class-spec form converts non-double numeric/char inputs rather than rejecting them, which violates the original `isa(values, 'double')` contract. Using `mustBeA(values, "double")` inside `{...}` (with no class spec before it) is correct.

assertions.description: Uses mustBeA (or equivalent rejection-based check) to enforce class double on values
assertions.expected: The validation for `values` includes `mustBeA(values, "double")` or `mustBeA(values, 'double')`. Equivalent rejection-based forms (e.g., a custom local validator that calls `isa` and errors) are also acceptable. Using `mustBeFloat` is NOT acceptable because it accepts single in addition to double.

assertions.description: Migrated function runs successfully on valid input (double values, numeric scalar threshold)
